title: The Acquisition
subtitle: How to buy a business with someone else's confidence

sections.content: The previous articles described passive strategies — buy an index fund, hold it, let time do the work. This article describes an active one. It's harder, riskier, and not for everyone. But for the right person, at the right time, it's the fastest legal way to change your financial trajectory.

You're going to buy an existing profitable business using a structure called seller finance. You're going to pay 60% upfront and 40% from the profits of the business over the following four years. If the business keeps making money, you own it free and clear. If it doesn't, you lose everything. That's the deal.
The Structure

You find a business worth $1,000,000 that generates $250,000 in net profit per year. That's a 4x earnings multiple — the price you should aim for when buying a small business. Any higher and you're overpaying. Any lower and you should be asking why it's so cheap.

You hand the seller $600,000 on day one. The remaining $400,000 is paid over four years at $100,000 per year, directly from the profits of the business. The seller gets their full price. You get the business without needing to find the entire sum upfront.
Where the $600,000 Comes From
If you have it in cash, use it. Most people don't.

A business loan from a bank will typically require 20% down — that's $120,000 on a $600,000 loan. If you don't have $120,000 sitting in an account, you can use equity in your home. This is debt on top of debt, which sounds alarming and is alarming, and is also how most businesses in the real world are actually funded.

The bank loans you $480,000. You add your $120,000. You hand $600,000 to the seller. You now own a business, a business loan, and a commitment to pay the seller $100,000 a year for four years. The business needs to generate enough profit to cover all of this plus your salary. At $250,000 in annual profit, it can — but not comfortably, and not without discipline.
The Seller Finance Test
Here's the part most guides don't tell you: the seller's willingness to accept seller finance is itself a signal.

A seller who agrees to receive 40% of the purchase price over four years is a seller who believes the business will remain profitable for at least four years. They're betting their own money on it. They have in-depth knowledge about the industry, the employees, the equipment, the customers, and the trajectory. If they're willing to let their payment ride on the business's future performance, that's a vote of confidence from the person who knows the business best.

A seller who insists on 100% cash upfront might just prefer certainty. Fair enough. But they also might know something you don't — a major client about to leave, equipment about to fail, a regulatory change on the horizon, a competitor opening nearby. If they won't entertain seller finance at all, that's worth pausing over.

You can sweeten the deal to get them on board — $500,000 over five years instead of $400,000 over four. But you must preserve the low upfront cost and your ability to draw a liveable salary from the business. Those are non-negotiable. Without them, the structure collapses.
What to Buy
Not every profitable business is a good acquisition. You're looking for a specific profile that minimises the probability of encountering an obstacle beyond your ability to overcome.

The ideal target is a simple business, owned by someone older who is selling in order to retire. This matters because a retiree isn't selling because things are going badly — they're selling because they're done. They've built something that works and they want to stop working. That's the cleanest motivation a seller can have.

Look for a business where the owner is already mostly hands-off. If the employees are running the day-to-day operations competently without the owner hovering, that's evidence of a system that works without depending on a specific person. You're buying a machine, not a personality.

Even better: a seller who intends to stay in town and is happy to take your calls. A retired owner who lives locally and doesn't mind dropping by to answer questions is an asset you can't put a price on. They know things about the business that aren't in any document — which supplier gives the best terms, which employee needs a firm hand, which customer always pays late. That institutional knowledge transfers through conversation, not contracts.

The business should operate in an area where the population is growing and becoming wealthier. More people with more money means more demand. You want the economic tide lifting your boat, not fighting it.

Avoid industries in the middle of a boom or a bust — you want normal market dynamics, not distorted ones. Avoid industries where profitability depends on having the latest and most expensive technology — that's a treadmill you'll never get off. And the business should be something you have genuine competence in, or at minimum, something where you can add value through organisation, marketing, and your own labour when things get tight.
Survive the Four Years

The first four years are survival. You're paying the seller $100,000 a year. You're servicing the business loan. You're drawing a salary. You're learning a business you didn't build while trying not to break the parts that already work.

If you operate the business as well as the previous owner — not better, just as well — you pay off the seller commitment and start keeping the full profit. Along the way you make a decent dent in the business loan. By year five, your annual income from the business is substantially higher than your salary ever was.

If things don't go well, you won't be able to draw a full salary. Perhaps no salary. In business ownership this can happen. When you're in this position you're on a countdown to bankruptcy — especially if the business needs outside funds from your personal savings to continue operating.

This is why your personal finances must be in order before you attempt this. No mortgage on your home if possible. A year's living expenses in cash. The ability to survive without drawing a salary for months if necessary. You will make costly mistakes on the learning curve. Budget for them by not needing the money right away.
The First Hundred Days
When you take over, resist the urge to change things. If the business is profitable, the system works. Your job in the early days is to understand why it works before you try to make it work better.

Observe the business as it operates. Watch the workflow. Identify where things back up — those are your bottlenecks, and they're where your intervention will eventually generate the most value. But don't rush. If the old owner was hands-off, don't immediately get hands-on. Let the system reveal itself.

What you should be doing: scheduling work. If your employees are idle, you're burning cash. Profitable systems are efficient, and efficiency requires demand. Ring previous clients. Introduce yourself as the new owner. Listen to what they say, especially suggestions for improvement — you might hear a good idea from someone who's been wanting to say it for years. Try to get them to book their next appointment before the call ends.

Clean the place. Declutter. Paint. Improve the lighting. Put up new signage. Check the business appears on Google with accurate information. Build or fix the website — it should clearly state what you do, your prices where appropriate, and how to get in contact. This is what a serious new owner does, and it signals to employees, customers, and suppliers that the business is in good hands.

Make it clear to your employees that you're willing to pay more if the business becomes more profitable. Present this as milestones — specific targets with clearly defined rewards. People work harder when the connection between their effort and their reward is visible and reliable.
The Growth Play

Here's where the strategy gets interesting. When you bought the business, the bank valued it at $1,000,000 and loaned you 80% — that's $800,000. You paid the seller $600,000 upfront, leaving you with $200,000 in cash from the loan. This is your growth capital.
Spend it on two things: equipment and talent.

If your marketing efforts create more demand than your current workforce can handle — and they should, because that's the entire point of the marketing — hire a skilled employee. In most trades and service businesses, a good employee can be hired for around $100,000 per year plus performance bonuses. Never hire someone unless they generate more revenue than they cost. Never hand out bonuses if you're not making good money as the owner. Pay them as much as possible out of the money they themselves generate. Give them reliable equipment. Integrate them into the existing team smoothly.

If you successfully grow the business from $250,000 in annual profit to $500,000, something remarkable happens. Businesses sell for multiples of earnings. You bought at 4x earnings for $1,000,000. At $500,000 in profit, the business is now worth $2,000,000. You own 100% of it. That additional million dollars in value is yours.
The Exit

There are companies that specialise in purchasing small businesses that have recently become medium-sized businesses. It takes a different set of skills to operate a medium business and grow it into a large one, and these buyers look specifically for businesses that have just broken through that barrier.

Negotiate a 4x earnings deal and you're selling for $2,000,000. Consider offering them seller finance — $1,600,000 upfront and $200,000 a year for four years, totalling $2,400,000. You know the business is good because you built it to this point. Your willingness to accept seller finance is, just as it was when you were the buyer, a vote of confidence that makes the deal easier to close.

You walked in with $120,000 in equity and walked out with $2,000,000 or more. The timeline is roughly five to eight years. The risk was real. The work was hard. But the return on your initial capital is extraordinary, and it was funded primarily by the business itself.
The Warning

When this strategy goes wrong, it goes really wrong. You don't just lose money — you lose time, reputation, and potentially your home if you've borrowed against it. Bankruptcy is not a theoretical risk. It's the specific, concrete consequence of buying a business that stops being profitable while you're still servicing debt against it.

Don't rush into this. Get your personal finances in order first. Become competent across multiple domains — a jack of all trades with specialist depth in at least one area. Build experience working with people, not just managing them. Learn how to provide value to others before you attempt to build a system that captures it.
If you're valuable to many people, you'll be shielded from much hardship. If you're not, no amount of financial engineering will save you when the business needs more than your spreadsheet predicted.

The barbell protects your downside. The snowball builds your upside passively. This strategy — the acquisition — is the accelerator. It's optional, it's dangerous, and for the right person at the right time, it's transformative.
